title: Prompt Engineering: How Prompt Vocabulary affects Domain Knowledge
author: Dimitri Schreiter
organization: Georg-August-Universität Göttingen, Institute of Computer Science
extraction_notes: Master's thesis on prompt specificity. Tested 4 LLMs (Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct, Granite-13B-Instruct-V2, Flan-T5-XL, Mistral-Large 2) across MMLU, GPQA, GSM8K. Developed synonymization framework to systematically substitute nouns, verbs, adjectives at varying specificity levels. Key finding: optimal specificity range exists — not 'more is always better.'
key_claims: There exists an optimal specificity range across all considered models where the LLM performs best
Generally increasing the specificity of prompts does not have a significant impact beyond the optimal range
Increasing verb specificity beyond the optimal range results in significantly negative effects for reasoning tasks
Noun specificity impact is less pronounced than verb specificity
Tested on Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct, Granite-13B-Instruct-V2, Flan-T5-XL, and Mistral-Large 2
Evaluated across MMLU (STEM, law, medicine), GPQA, and GSM8K datasets
Word Sense Disambiguation used to ensure semantically appropriate synonym selection
Synonyms categorized into low, intermediate, and high specificity using WordNet taxonomy depth
